new_classes.definition_en: Natural or processed water intended for human consumption or industrial use, classified under CN heading 2201, including natural mineral water, sparkling water, ordinary natural water, and ice and snow.

Natural mineral water and artificially carbonated (aerated) water classified under CN subheading 2201 10, as described in HS Explanatory Notes to heading 2201, paragraphs B and C. Excludes natural mineral water presented in spray cans for skin care (heading 3304).
A quality borne by a water or beverage entity indicating that it contains dissolved carbon dioxide, rendering it sparkling or effervescent.

A quality borne by a water entity indicating that it originates from a natural underground source, is microbiologically wholesome, and has a constant mineral composition, as recognised under the HS Explanatory Notes to heading 2201 paragraph B.

coverage_explanation: The proposed axioms capture the two main product-discriminating criteria for CN 2201 10: (1) the natural mineral water character (modelled via NaturalMineralWaterQuality) and (2) the carbonated/aerated water character (modelled via CarbonatedQuality). The parent class Water (heading 2201) is introduced as a new class under NonAlcoholicBeverage to anchor the hierarchy correctly. The explicit exclusion of natural mineral water in spray cans for skin care (heading 3304) cannot be fully represented in OWL DL without introducing a context-dependent use/presentation property that goes beyond the current TBox; this exclusion is noted in coverage_explanation but not formally axiomatised. Additionally, the note refers to HS Explanatory Notes paragraphs B and C, which contain detailed criteria (e.g. recognised bottling conditions, specific mineral thresholds) that cannot be mechanically captured in OWL without further TBox extensions. The disjunction between the mineral water and carbonated water sub-categories is expressed as an inclusive disjunction (either-or or both), which matches the legal text grouping both under 2201 10. Coverage is reduced accordingly for the unmodelled exclusion and the detailed HS criteria.
